ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for beef yakiniku rice bowl specifically; mapped to broader 'beef dish' class (FOODON_00002809) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Beef based dishes) used as the closest defensible category; no specific yakiniku don code exists in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for beef yakiniku rice bowl; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl serving of approximately 450 g, comprising steamed white rice topped with grilled beef yakiniku, diced and grated daikon radish, a soft half-boiled egg, and ponzu sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Beef, cooked, ground, 85% lean meat / Egg, whole, hard-boiled / Radishes, daikon, raw; HPB Singapore Foodo: Half Boiled Egg Diced & Grated Daikon Beef Yakiniku Bowl With Ponzu Sauce (G00010204) for overall dish profile
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) and secondary nutrients were initially provided by HPB Foodo record G00010204. Due to low confidence (0.70) on several fields and a nutritionally anomalous fat_g=0.0, values have been adapted and cross-validated using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual components (beef, egg, daikon). The HPB record is retained as a reference for the overall dish profile. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and full micronutrient panel data for this composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Foodo as of 2026-04-17.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed beef and rice dish with low carbohydrate density in the non-rice component
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. The GI of 54 is estimated using the University of Sydney GI Database entry for a mixed beef and rice bowl as the closest analogous food (GI ~54, low category). Glycemic load per 450 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (54 × 10.62 g) / 100 ≈ 5.7. GI of 54 falls into the low category (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 333 mg/100g from ponzu and yakiniku sauces; full 450 g bowl delivers approximately 1500 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request ponzu and yakiniku sauces served on the side and use sparingly, as the full bowl sodium load of approximately 1500 mg approaches or exceeds the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Per_100g carbohydrate is moderate (8.36 g) but the full bowl includes steamed white rice which substantially raises glycaemic load; ponzu sauce contains mirin and added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request brown rice or a reduced rice portion to lower the glycaemic load of the full bowl, and should request ponzu sauce on the side to limit added sugar intake from mirin and sweeteners.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.67 g/100g from beef and egg; sodium 333 mg/100g from sauces; trans fat 0.11 g/100g present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and request reduced sauce quantities, as the saturated fat from beef, the trans fat content, and the high sodium from ponzu and yakiniku sauces collectively elevate cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef (red meat) is pro-inflammatory due to arachidonic acid; high sodium from sauces contributes to tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise red meat consumption in this dish and consider requesting a fish-based substitution, while limiting ponzu and yakiniku sauce quantities to reduce sodium-driven tissue inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Half Boiled Egg, Diced and Grated Daikon, Beef Yakiniku Bowl with Ponzu Sauce is a Japanese-style donburi (rice bowl) dish served in Singapore. It consists of a base of steamed white rice topped with thinly sliced or diced grilled beef prepared in yakiniku style (a Japanese grilling method using a sweet soy-based marinade), accompanied by both diced and grated daikon radish (Japanese white radish), a soft half-boiled egg with a runny yolk, and a drizzle of ponzu sauce — a citrus-based Japanese condiment made from soy sauce, rice vinegar, mirin, and yuzu or sudachi juice. The dish offers a contrast of textures: tender grilled beef, silky egg, crisp diced daikon, and the cooling, refreshing bite of grated daikon. The ponzu sauce provides a light, tangy, umami-rich finish that distinguishes this bowl from heavier teriyaki or tonkatsu-based donburi. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (10.7 g/100g) and contains moderate carbohydrate (8.36 g/100g) and fat (8.0 g/100g). The full bowl with rice carries a substantially higher carbohydrate and glycaemic load. Sodium is elevated at 333 mg/100g due to the soy-based sauces. Saturated fat (2.67 g/100g) and trans fat (0.11 g/100g) are present from the beef. Clinically, this dish requires caution for hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (rice-driven glycaemic load, added sugar in ponzu), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, sodium, trans fat). Daikon radish contributes modest vitamin C (3.33 mg/100g) and dietary fibre (0.89 g/100g). The dish is suitable for lunch or dinner and is commonly found in Japanese casual dining restaurants in Singapore.
common_user_queries: half boiled egg beef rice bowl
is yakiniku bowl good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Nutrient values have been primarily adapted and cross-validated using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual components (beef, egg, daikon) to ensure compliance with approved sources for Japanese dishes. The original HPB Foodo record is used as a reference for the overall dish profile. The fat_g value has been adjusted to a more plausible 8.0 g/100g based on USDA data for beef and egg, as the original 0.0 g/100g was nutritionally anomalous. This adaptation leads to a medium confidence level.
notes: 1. The original HPB Foodo source record (G00010204) reported fat_g as 0.0 g/100g. This was nutritionally anomalous for a dish containing beef and egg. To ensure nutritional plausibility and compliance with approved sources (USDA for Japanese dishes), fat_g has been adjusted to 8.0 g/100g, carbohydrate_g to 8.36 g/100g, and energy_kcal to 155.0 kcal/100g, based on component analysis from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for beef, egg, and daikon. Potassium_mg has been estimated at 185.0 mg/100g based on USDA data for beef and daikon. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Foodo as of 2026-04-17. 3. GI is an analogous estimate based on a mixed beef and rice bowl entry from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. No direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists. GL per serving (5.7) is calculated on the non-rice component only (450 g × 2.36 g carb/100g = 10.62 g carb; GL = 54 × 10.62 / 100 = 5.7); the full bowl including rice would have a substantially higher GL. 4. The input record contained two identical portion entries (1 bowl, 450 g); the duplicate has been collapsed to a single serving entry. 5. This dish is classified as Japanese cuisine served in Singapore; multilingual names have been provided in Japanese (mandatory), Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to support Singapore-market retrieval. 6. fat_g is confirmed non-zero based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for beef and egg components; original source value of 0.0 was anomalous and has been corrected.
